name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ قَدْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ وَوَٰعَدْنَـٰكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنَ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ (٨٠)
```

```english
O Children of Israel! We delivered you from your enemy, and We appointed with you a tryst on the right side of the Mount and We sent down to you manna and quails: (80)
```

```arabic
كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِى ۖ وَمَن يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِى فَقَدْ هَوَىٰ (٨١)
```

```english
‘Eat of the good things We have provided you, but do not overstep the bounds therein, lest My wrath should descend on you, and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes. (81)
```

```arabic
وَإِنِّى لَغَفَّارٌ لِّمَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا ثُمَّ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (٨٢)
```

```english
Indeed I forgive those who repent, become faithful, act righteously, and follow guidance.’ (82)
```

```arabic
۞ وَمَآ أَعْجَلَكَ عَن قَوْمِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٨٣)
```

```english
[God said,] ‘O Moses, what has prompted you to hasten ahead of your people?’ (83)
```

```arabic
قَالَ هُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ عَلَىٰٓ أَثَرِى وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضَىٰ (٨٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘They are close upon my heels, and I hurried on to You, my Lord, that You may be pleased.’ (84)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (٨٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘Indeed We tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray.’ (85)
```

```arabic
فَرَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا ۚ أَفَطَالَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْعَهْدُ أَمْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُم مَّوْعِدِى (٨٦)
```

```english
Thereupon Moses returned to his people, indignant and grieved. He said, ‘O my people! Did your Lord not give you a true promise? Did the period [of my absence] seem too long to you? Or did you desire that your Lord’s wrath should descend on you and so you failed your tryst with me?’ (86)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَخْلَفْنَا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنَا وَلَـٰكِنَّا حُمِّلْنَآ أَوْزَارًا مِّن زِينَةِ ٱلْقَوْمِ فَقَذَفْنَـٰهَا فَكَذَٰلِكَ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (٨٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘We did not fail our tryst with you of our own accord, but we were laden with the weight of those people’s ornaments, and we cast them [into the fire] and so did the Samiri.’ (87)
```

```arabic
فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ وَإِلَـٰهُ مُوسَىٰ فَنَسِىَ (٨٨)
```

```english
Then he produced for them a calf—a [lifeless] body with a low—and they said, This is your god and the god of Moses, so he forgot! (88)
```

```arabic
أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَلَّا يَرْجِعُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا (٨٩)
```

```english
Did they not see that it did not answer them, nor could it bring them any benefit or harm? (89)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ قَالَ لَهُمْ هَـٰرُونُ مِن قَبْلُ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا فُتِنتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى وَأَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرِى (٩٠)
```

```english
Aaron had certainly told them earlier, ‘O my people! You are only being tested by it. Indeed your Lord is the All-beneficent. So follow me and obey my command!’ (90)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ لَن نَّبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عَـٰكِفِينَ حَتَّىٰ يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ (٩١)
```

```english
They had said, ‘We will keep on clinging to it until Moses returns to us.’ (91)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰهَـٰرُونُ مَا مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوٓا۟ (٩٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘O Aaron! What kept you, when you saw them going astray, (92)
```

```arabic
أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ ۖ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى (٩٣)
```

```english
from following me? Did you disobey my command?’ (93)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَبْنَؤُمَّ لَا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِى وَلَا بِرَأْسِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى خَشِيتُ أَن تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِى (٩٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘O son of my mother! Do not grab my beard or my head! I feared lest you should say, ‘‘You have caused a rift among the Children of Israel and did not heed my word [of advice].’’’ (94)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكَ يَـٰسَـٰمِرِىُّ (٩٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘What is your business, O Samiri?’ (95)
```

```arabic
قَالَ بَصُرْتُ بِمَا لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِى نَفْسِى (٩٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘I saw what they did not see. I took a handful [of dust] from the messenger’s trail and threw it. That is how my soul prompted me.’ (96)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَٱذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ أَن تَقُولَ لَا مِسَاسَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّن تُخْلَفَهُۥ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِكَ ٱلَّذِى ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عَاكِفًا ۖ لَّنُحَرِّقَنَّهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَنسِفَنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ نَسْفًا (٩٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘Begone! It shall be your [lot] throughout life to say, ‘‘Do not touch me!’’ Indeed, there is a tryst for you which you will not fail to keep! Now look at your god to whom you went on clinging. We will burn it down and then scatter it[s ashes] into the sea. (97)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا (٩٨)
```

```english
Indeed your God is Allah. There is no god except Him. He embraces all things in [His] knowledge.’ (98)
```

## (Commentary)

The final section of the story of Moses (عليه السلام) presented in this Surah recounts several of the favors Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel, such as saving them from their enemy, the appointment at the right side of Mount *Tur* (الطُّور), and the sending down of manna and quails. It concludes by mentioning the story of the Samiri and his leading the people astray through the worship of the calf—a story connected to the appointment at Mount *Tur*.

This part of the section—which contains an explanation of the Children of Israel exposing themselves to the wrath of the Exalted—is the primary objective of this chapter. For this reason, the discourse details it extensively, while other matters are mentioned only through brief allusion and summary.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ قَدْ أَنْجَيْناكُمْ مِنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ** — "O Children of Israel, We delivered you from your enemy" (20:80) to the end of the verse. It is as if the speech implies a preceding command of "saying," meaning: We said, "O Children of Israel..." His saying: **قَدْ أَنْجَيْناكُمْ مِنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ** ("We delivered you from your enemy") refers to Pharaoh, whom Allah drowned, thereby saving the Children of Israel from him after a long period of tribulation.

His saying: **وَواعَدْناكُمْ جانِبَ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنَ** — "and We made an appointment with you at the right side of the Mount" (20:80). The word "right" (أَيْمَنَ) is in the accusative case as an adjective for "side" (جانِب). Perhaps what is meant by this appointment is the forty-night appointment of Moses for the revelation of the Torah. This story has already been discussed in Surah al-Baqarah and elsewhere, as has the story of the sending down of manna and quails.

His saying: **كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّباتِ ما رَزَقْناكُمْ** — "Eat of the good things We have provided you" (20:81) is a permission (إباحة) expressed in the form of a command. The annexation of "good things" (طَيِّباتِ) to "what We have provided you" (ما رَزَقْناكُمْ) is the annexation of an adjective to the noun it describes. This is because it would be meaningless for Allah to attribute provision to Himself and then divide it into "good" and "not good," as supported by His saying in another place: **وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ** — "and provided them with good things" (45:16).

His saying: **وَلا تَطْغَوْا فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِي** — "and do not overstep the bounds therein, lest My wrath should descend upon you" (20:81). The pronoun "therein" (فِيهِ) refers to the act of eating related to the "good things." This overstepping occurs through ingratitude for the blessing and failing to give thanks, as they said: **يا مُوسى لَنْ نَصْبِرَ عَلى طَعامٍ واحِدٍ فَادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ يُخْرِجْ لَنا مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْ بَقْلِها وَقِثَّائِها وَفُومِها وَعَدَسِها وَبَصَلِها** — "O Moses, we will not put up with one kind of food. So invoke your Lord for us, so that He may bring forth for us of what the earth grows—its greens and its cucumbers, its garlic, its lentils, and its onions" (2:61).

His saying: **فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِي** ("lest My wrath should descend upon you") means My wrath becomes due and necessary. It is derived from the "falling due" (حُلول) of a debt (الدين), which is said to "fall due" (يحل) when its repayment becomes obligatory. Wrath (الغضب), among the active attributes (صفاته الفعلية) of the Exalted, is manifested as His will to inflict harm upon a servant by preparing the causes for that as a result of a sin committed.

His saying: **وَمَنْ يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِي فَقَدْ هَوى** — "and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes" (20:81). That is, he falls, from the word *al-huwi* (الهوي) meaning to fall, and it has been interpreted as destruction (الهلاك).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** — "Indeed I am most forgiving toward him who repents, believes, and acts righteously, and then follows guidance" (20:82). This is a promise of emphasized mercy following a severe warning. For this reason, He described Himself with the intensive form of forgiveness, saying: **وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ** ("Indeed I am most forgiving"), rather than saying "I am the Forgiver" or "I shall forgive."

Repentance (التوبة), which is "returning" (الرجوع), can be from sin to obedience, just as it can be from polytheism (الشرك) to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. Similarly, faith (الإيمان) can be in Allah, just as it can be in the signs (آيات) of Allah, including His prophets, messengers, and every ruling they brought from Allah the Exalted. The use of "faith" in the Quran is frequent in both senses, as is the use of "repentance" in both aforementioned senses. The Children of Israel were both embroiled in sins through which they became transgressors (فسقوا) and embroiled in polytheism, such as the worship of the calf. Therefore, there is no reason to divert the speech from its apparent generality regarding repentance from both polytheism and sin, and faith in Allah and His signs. Likewise, it remains general regarding the repentant and the believers among the Children of Israel and others, even if the Children of Israel were the immediate subjects of the address; for divine attributes like forgiveness are not restricted to one people over another.

Thus, the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: "I am indeed abundantly forgiving to every human being who repents and believes, whether they repent from polytheism or from sin, and whether they believe in Me or in My signs through My messengers or the rulings they brought, by regretting what they did and performing righteous deeds (عملاً صالحاً) by replacing opposition and rebellion in what they disobeyed with obedience therein." This fulfills the essential meaning of "returning" from something. A detailed discussion on this was provided in the commentary on His saying: **إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ** — "Repentance [accepted] by Allah is only for..." (4:17) in the fourth volume of this book.

As for His saying: **ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** ("and then follows guidance"), guidance (الاهتداء) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — stands in contrast to straying (الضلال), as evidenced by His saying: **مَنِ اهْتَدى فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها** — "Whoever is guided is only guided for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, only goes astray to its detriment" (17:15), and His saying: **لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ** — "he who errs cannot harm you if you are guided" (5:105). Does it mean that he should not stray in the very matter he repented from by returning to the sin again—thereby implying that repentance for a sin only benefits what was committed before the repentance and does not suffice if he returns to it a second time? Or does it mean that he should not stray in other matters—thereby implying that forgiveness only benefits him regarding the sin he repented for if he does not stray in other deeds? Or is it intended to encompass both meanings?

The apparent use of the conjunction "then" (ثُمَّ) suggests the first meaning, implying steadfastness (الثبات) and uprightness (الاستقامة) in repentance. This returns to the condition of "rectification" (الإصلاح) mentioned in several verses, such as His saying: **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — "excepting those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (3:89, 24:5).

However, two issues remain with the verse under this interpretation: First, the point of using the intensive form "Most Forgiving" (الغفار), which denotes abundance. What is the meaning of the abundance of His forgiveness for someone who committed a single sin and then repented?

Second, its implication would be that whoever violates one of His rulings is a disbeliever in Him, even if he acknowledges that it is from Allah and only disobeys out of following passion (اتباع الهوى) rather than rejecting the ruling—unless it is said that the verse, by including the phrase "repents and believes" (تابَ وَآمَنَ), only includes the polytheist or the one who rejects a divine ruling, which is problematic as you can see.

Thus, it can be said: Repentance and faith here mean repentance from polytheism and faith in Allah, as these are the two meanings intended in most places in His speech where repentance and faith are mentioned together. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: **وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً** ("and acts righteously") would be obedience to His rulings by complying with His commands and refraining from His prohibitions. The meaning of the verse would then be: "Whoever repents from polytheism, believes in Allah, and performs the duties He has obligated, I am abundantly forgiving of his misdeeds; I forgive him slip after slip, so the forgiveness becomes abundant due to the abundance of its instances."

Allah has mentioned a similar meaning—the forgiveness of misdeeds—in His saying: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31).

**Therefore, His saying:** **وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً** corresponds to the verse in Surah an-Nisa, but there remains an additional condition that restricts the ruling of forgiveness, which is the meaning of His saying: **ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** ("and then follows guidance"). This is the "finding of the way" (الاهتداء إلى الطريق). It appears that forgiveness is only granted to the believer who performs righteous deeds if he seeks it through its proper way and enters it through its proper door.

We do not find in His speech anything that restricts faith in Allah and righteous deeds in their effect and acceptance before Allah except faith in the Messenger—in the sense of submission (التسليم) to him, obeying him in matters great and small, taking the religion from him, following the path he outlines, and adhering to him without independence or innovation (ابتداع) that leads to following the footsteps of Satan. In short, it is his guardianship (الولاية) over the believers in their religious and worldly affairs. Allah has legislated his guardianship, made his obedience mandatory, and obligated taking from him and following his example in very many verses—there is no need to cite them all, nor is there space to exhaust them. "The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves."

Most of the Children of Israel, despite their faith in Allah and their belief in the messengership of Moses and Aaron, were hesitant regarding their guardianship—or acted as if they were hesitant—as is explicit in the general accounts of them in the Book of Allah. Perhaps this is the reason for the occurrence of the verse—**وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى**—after they were forbidden from overstepping bounds and warned of Allah's wrath.

It has thus become clear that the meaning of "guidance" (الاهتداء) in the verse, according to what other verses indicate, is faith in the Messenger by following him in matters of religion and the world. In other words, it is being guided to his guardianship (الولاية).

By this, the status of what has been said regarding the interpretation of **ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** becomes clear. It has been said: Guidance is the necessity of faith and its continuation throughout life. It has been said: That he should not doubt his faith again. It has been said: Adhering to the Sunnah of the Prophet and not following the path of innovation. It has been said: Guidance is to know that his work has a reward for which he will be recompensed. It has been said: It is the purification of the heart from blameworthy traits. It has been said: It is the preservation of the creed from opposing the truth in anything—for guidance in this sense is distinct from faith and distinct from action. What is sought in all these views is to interpret guidance in a sense that does not return to faith and righteous deeds, yet there is no evidence for any of what they mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَعْجَلَكَ عَنْ قَوْمِكَ يا مُوسى** — "‘O Moses, what has made you hasten from your people?’" (20:83) to His saying: **لِتَرْضى** — "‘...so that You may be pleased.’" (20:84). This is a narration of a conversation that took place between Him, the Exalted, and Moses (عليه السلام) at the appointment of Mount *Tur*, where the Torah was revealed, as recounted in detail in Surah al-A'raf.

The apparent context is that it is a question about the reason that necessitated Moses to hasten ahead of his people and attend the appointment at the Mount before them. It was as if it were expected that they would all attend the Mount together, but Moses preceded them and left them behind. So it was said to him: **وَما أَعْجَلَكَ عَنْ قَوْمِكَ يا مُوسى** ("‘O Moses, what has made you hasten from your people?’"). He replied: **هُمْ أُولاءِ عَلى أَثَرِي** — "‘They are close on my heels,’" (20:84) meaning they are traveling behind me and will catch up shortly. **وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضى** — "‘and I hastened to You, my Lord, so that You may be pleased.’" (20:84). That is, the reason for my haste is to attain Your pleasure, O Lord.

It appears that the "people" (القوم) mentioned as being "on his heels" are the seventy men he chose for the appointment with his Lord. For the fact that he left Aaron in charge of his people after him, along with other aspects of the story and his later saying: **أَفَطالَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْعَهْدُ** ("‘Did the period [of my absence] seem too long to you?’"), indicates that it was not intended for all the Children of Israel to attend the Mount.

This address could have been directed to Moses (عليه السلام) at the beginning of his arrival at the appointment, just as it could have been at the end of his time there. For the question about the calf is different from the calf itself, which coincides with the journey and the meeting. If the question was not at the very beginning of his arrival, then His subsequent saying: **فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنْ بَعْدِكَ** ("‘Indeed We have tried your people in your absence...’") remains consistent, based on the premise that the trial (الفتنة) occurred after they deemed Moses's absence too long, according to the reports. There is no need for forced interpretations to reconcile the verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنْ بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ السَّامِرِيُّ** — "He said, ‘Indeed We have tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray.’" (20:85). Trial (الفتنة) means examination and testing. The attribution of leading astray to the Samiri—who cast the calf and brought it out for them, so they worshipped it and went astray—is because he was one of the active causes in it.

The "so" (الفاء) in His saying: **فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ** is for causation (التعليل), explaining what is understood from the preceding speech. What is understood from Moses's saying: **هُمْ أُولاءِ عَلى أَثَرِي** ("‘They are close on my heels’") is that his people were in a good state and nothing had occurred among them to cause concern. So it was as if it were said: Do not be confident in the state you left them in, for We have tried them and they have gone astray.

His saying: **قَوْمَكَ** ("your people") uses a noun where a pronoun could have been used. Perhaps the "people" intended here is different from those intended in the previous verse; those here being the general populace, while those there were the seventy men Moses chose for the appointment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَرَجَعَ مُوسى إِلى قَوْمِهِ غَضْبانَ أَسِفاً** — "So Moses returned to his people, angry and sorrowful." (20:86) to His saying: **فَأَخْلَفْتُمْ مَوْعِدِي** — "‘...and you failed in [your] promise to me?’" (20:86). "Angry" (الغضبان) is an adjective derived from anger (الغضب), as is "sorrowful" (الأسف) from *al-asaf* (الأسف), which means grief and intense anger. "Promise" (الموعد) is the pledge. Their failing in their promise to him was their abandonment of what they had promised him of good conduct in his absence until his return. This is supported by His saying in another place: **بِئْسَما خَلَفْتُمُونِي مِنْ بَعْدِي** — "‘Evil is that which you have done in my absence!’" (7:150).

The meaning is: Moses returned to his people in a state of intense anger—or grief—and began to blame them for what they had done. He said, "O my people, did your Lord not give you a good promise?"—which was to send down the Torah containing Allah's rulings, in the adherence to which lies their happiness in this world and the hereafter—or His promise to save them from their enemy, establish them in the land, and favor them with His great blessings. **أَفَطالَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْعَهْدُ** ("‘Did the period [of my absence] seem too long to you?’")—referring to the duration of Moses's separation from them, such that they despaired of his return and their order became disrupted? **أَمْ أَرَدْتُمْ أَنْ يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** ("‘Or did you desire that wrath from your Lord should descend upon you?’")—so you overstepped by disbelieving in Him after having faith and worshipped the calf? **فَأَخْلَفْتُمْ مَوْعِدِي** ("‘and you failed in [your] promise to me?’")—and you abandoned what you promised me of good conduct in my absence.

Other meanings have been suggested for His saying: **فَأَخْلَفْتُمْ مَوْعِدِي**:
Some said their failing his promise was that he commanded them to catch up with him, but they abandoned traveling on his heels. Others said it was that he commanded them to obey Aaron after him until his return, but they disobeyed him—among other views.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا ما أَخْلَفْنا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنا** — "They said, ‘We did not fail in our promise to you of our own accord...’" (20:87) to the end of the verse. *Al-malk* (الملك) is a verbal noun meaning "to possess" or "to control." It is as if they meant by their saying: **ما أَخْلَفْنا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنا**: "We did not oppose you while we possessed any control over our affair"—as has been said. It is also possible that the meaning is: "We did not spend any of our own wealth in the casting of the calf such that we would be intentional and deliberate in this matter; rather, we were carrying loads of the people's jewelry, so we threw them, and the Samiri took them and cast them into the fire, and out came the calf."

"Loads" (الأوزار) is the plural of *wizr* (الوزر), meaning a heavy weight. "Jewelry" (الزينة) refers to ornaments like necklaces, earrings, and bracelets. "Casting" (القذف), "throwing" (الإلقاء), and "flinging" (النبذ) are near-synonyms meaning to toss or throw.

The meaning of His saying: **وَلكِنَّا حُمِّلْنا أَوْزاراً** etc., is: "But we had with us heavy loads of the people's jewelry"—perhaps meaning the people of Pharaoh—"so we threw them, and likewise the Samiri threw"—he threw what we had tossed into the fire, or he threw what he had just as we threw what we were carrying—and out came the calf.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ عِجْلاً جَسَداً لَهُ خُوارٌ فَقالُوا هذا إِلهُكُمْ وَإِلهُ مُوسى فَنَسِيَ** — "Then he produced for them a calf—a [hollow] body with a lowing sound. Then they said, ‘This is your god and the god of Moses, but he has forgotten!’" (20:88). The word "produced" (الإخراج) indicates that the manner of making the calf was hidden from the people, not in their sight, until he surprised them by revealing and showing it. "Body" (الجسد) refers to a corpse or form that has no soul; the term *jasad* is never applied to a living being with a soul. This is evidence that the calf had no soul and no life in it. "Lowing" (الخوار) is the sound of a calf.

Perhaps His saying: **فَكَذلِكَ أَلْقَى السَّامِرِيُّ فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ** etc., is taken as independent speech—either from the speech of Allah the Exalted, concluding the people's words at their saying: **فَقَذَفْناها** ("‘so we flung them’"), or as part of the people's speech. In the latter case, the pronoun in "they said" (قالُوا) refers to some of the people, and the pronoun in "he produced for them" (فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ) refers to others, as is apparent.

Regarding the pronoun in "he has forgotten" (فَنَسِيَ): It has been said it refers to Moses, meaning: They said, "This is your god and the god of Moses, but Moses has forgotten his god here and went to seek him at the Mount." It has also been said the pronoun refers to the Samiri, meaning his forgetting Allah after remembering and believing in Him; i.e., the Samiri forgot his Lord, so he did what he did and led the people astray.

The apparent meaning of His saying: **فَقالُوا هذا إِلهُكُمْ وَإِلهُ مُوسى** ("Then they said, ‘This is your god and the god of Moses...’"), where the saying is attributed to a group, is that there were those with the Samiri who assisted him in this matter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَلا يَرَوْنَ أَلَّا يَرْجِعُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلاً وَلا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً** — "Do they not see that it does not answer them a word, and it has no power to bring them any benefit or harm?" (20:89). This is a rebuke to them for worshipping it while they see that it does not return a word by responding to whoever calls upon it, nor does it possess for them any harm to ward it off, nor any benefit to bring and deliver to them. It is a necessity of their reason that the Lord must respond to whoever calls Him to ward off harm or bring benefit, and that He must possess harm and benefit for His subject.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ قالَ لَهُمْ هارُونُ مِنْ قَبْلُ يا قَوْمِ إِنَّما فُتِنْتُمْ بِهِ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ الرَّحْمنُ فَاتَّبِعُونِي وَأَطِيعُوا أَمْرِي** — "Aaron had certainly told them before, ‘O my people! You are only being tried by it. Indeed your Lord is the All-beneficent. So follow me and obey my command.’" (20:90). This emphasizes the rebuke and further establishes their crime. The meaning is: In addition to their failure to remember what the necessity of their reason reminded them of, and their failure to cease worshipping the calf through insight and intellect, they also paid no heed to what was addressed to them through hearing. For their prophet Aaron had certainly told them it was a trial by which they were being tested, and that their Lord is the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) — *the one whose mercy encompasses all creation* — and that it was obligatory for them to follow him and obey his command.

But they retorted to Aaron, saying: **لَنْ نَبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفِينَ حَتَّى يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنا مُوسى** — "They said, ‘We will not stop being devoted to it until Moses returns to us.’" (20:91). That is, they would persist in its worship until Moses returned to them, so they could see what he would say about it and what he would command them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ يا هارُونُ ما مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوا أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِي** — "[Moses] said, ‘O Aaron! What prevented you, when you saw them going astray, from following me? Did you disobey my command?’" (20:92). After speaking to the people regarding the matter of the calf, he (عليه السلام) turned to speak to his brother Aaron, as he was one of the three responsible parties in this tribulation. He had left him in charge over them and advised him when he was bidding him farewell, saying: **اخْلُفْنِي فِي قَوْمِي وَأَصْلِحْ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** — "‘Take my place among my people, and reform, and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption.’" (7:142).

It is as if His saying: "prevented you" (مَنَعَكَ) implies the meaning of "called you," i.e., "What called you to not follow me, acting as a barrier preventing you from following?" or "What prevented you, calling you to not follow me?" This is similar to His saying: **قالَ ما مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ** — "He said, ‘What prevented you from prostrating, when I commanded you?’" (7:12).

The meaning is: Moses said, rebuking Aaron: "What prevented you from following my way, which is to prevent them from straying and to be severe for the sake of Allah? Did you disobey my command to follow me and not follow the way of the agents of corruption?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ يَا بْنَ أُمَّ لا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِي وَلا بِرَأْسِي** etc. — "[Aaron] said, ‘O son of my mother! Do not seize my beard or my head!’" (20:94). "O son of my mother" (يَا بْنَ أُمَّ) was originally *ya bna ummi*, a term used to seek mercy and compassion, said to quiet Moses's anger. It appears from his saying: **لا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِي وَلا بِرَأْسِي** that he had seized his beard and head in anger to strike him, as reported in another place: **وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُ إِلَيْهِ** — "and he seized his brother by the head, pulling him toward himself" (7:150).

His saying: **إِنِّي خَشِيتُ أَنْ تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِي** — "‘Indeed I feared lest you should say, “You have caused a rift among the Children of Israel and did not heed my word.”’" (20:94). This is an explanation for a deleted premise indicated by the wording. The gist is: "Had I prevented them from worshipping the calf and resisted them to the utmost, only a few would have obeyed me, and that would have led to their splitting into two factions: a submissive believer and a rebellious polytheist. In that would have been the corruption of the people's state by replacing their apparent unity and agreement with division and discord, and it might have led to fighting. You had commanded me to reform when you said to me: ‘Reform, and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption.’ So I feared that you would say when you returned and witnessed the division and factionalism among the people: ‘You have caused a rift among the Children of Israel and did not heed my word.’" This was the excuse Aaron offered, and Moses accepted it and prayed for him and for himself, as in Surah al-A'raf: **رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِأَخِي وَأَدْخِلْنا فِي رَحْمَتِكَ وَأَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ** — "‘My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful.’" (7:151).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ فَما خَطْبُكَ يا سامِرِيُّ** — "[Moses] said, ‘And what is your business, O Samiri?’" (20:95). After finishing his conversation with his brother, he (عليه السلام) turned to speak to the Samiri, who was one of the three responsible parties and the one who led the people astray.

*Al-khatb* (الخطب) is a grave matter that concerns one. He says: "What is this grave matter that you have brought?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ بَصُرْتُ بِما لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُها وَكَذلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِي نَفْسِي** — "He said, ‘I saw what they did not see. I took a handful [of dust] from the messenger’s tracks and flung it [into the fire]. That is how my soul prompted me.’" (20:96). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Sight (البصر) is used for the seeing organ, as in His saying: **كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ** (‘like the twinkling of an eye’) and **وَإِذْ زاغَتِ الْأَبْصارُ** (‘when the eyes rolled’). It is also used for the power within it. The perceiving power of the heart is called insight (بصيرة) and sight (بصر), as in His saying: **فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** (‘We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today’) and **ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَما طَغى** (‘The eye did not swerve, nor did it overstep the bounds’). The plural of *basar* is *absar*, and the plural of *basirah* is *basa'ir*. The Exalted said: **فَما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَلا أَبْصارُهُمْ** (‘neither their hearing nor their sight availed them’). The organ is rarely called *basirah*. From the first sense, one says *absartu* (I saw), and from the second, *absartuhu* or *basurtu bihi* (I perceived it). One rarely says *basurtu* regarding the sense unless it is accompanied by the heart's vision." End quote.

Regarding His saying: **فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً** ("I took a handful"): It has been said that "handful" (القبضة) is a verbal noun used in the sense of the object (اسم المفعول). It was objected that when a verbal noun is used thus, the *ta* (ة) is not attached; one says "this is the weave (نسج) of Yemen," not "the weave-t (نسجه) of Yemen." Thus, it must be taken in the verse as a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق). This was countered by saying that what is forbidden is the attachment of the *ta* denoting limitation and once-ness, not merely for femininity as here. This is a circular argument, as there is no evidence that the *ta* here is for femininity.

Regarding His saying: **مِنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ** ("from the messenger’s tracks"): Track (الأثر) is the shape of the foot of a passerby on the road after passing. Its original meaning is what remains of a thing after it in a way that indicates it, like a building being the track of the builder, a product the track of the maker, and knowledge the track of the scholar. Of this type are the tracks of feet on the ground from passersby.

The "messenger" (الرسول) is one who carries a message. In the Quran, it is applied to the human messenger who carries Allah's message to the people, and it is applied with this word to Gabriel, the angel of revelation: **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ** — "it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger" (81:19). Likewise, it is applied to a group of angels as messengers: **بَلى وَرُسُلُنا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ** — "Yes, and Our messengers are with them, writing down" (43:80), and He also said regarding the angels: **جاعِلِ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلاً أُولِي أَجْنِحَةٍ** — "the maker of the angels [His] messengers, possessed of wings" (35:1).

The verse contains the Samiri's answer to what Moses (عليه السلام) asked him: **فَما خَطْبُكَ يا سامِرِيُّ** ("‘And what is your business, O Samiri?’"). This was a question about the reality of that grave matter he had done and what prompted him to do it. The context testifies that his saying: **وَكَذلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِي نَفْسِي** ("‘That is how my soul prompted me’") is his answer regarding the cause that called him to it and prompted him, and that the prompting of his soul was the motive for his action. As for the explanation of the reality of what he made, it is what he points to by saying: **بَصُرْتُ بِما لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ** ("‘I saw what they did not see. I took a handful from the messenger’s tracks...’"). We do not find in His speech in this story, nor in what is related to it in general, anything that clarifies what is meant by it; hence, they differed in its interpretation.

The majority interpreted it, in accordance with some narrations reported in the story, that the Samiri saw Gabriel when he descended to Moses for revelation, or saw him when he descended riding a horse from Paradise in front of Pharaoh and his troops when they entered the sea and were drowned. So he took a handful of dust from his footprint or the track of his horse's hoof. A special property of this dust was that it was not cast upon anything except that life entered it and a soul was infused into it. So he kept the dust until he made the calf, then he cast the dust into it, and it became alive, moved, and lowed.

Thus, the meaning of his saying: **بَصُرْتُ بِما لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ** ("‘I saw what they did not see’") is his seeing Gabriel when he descended on foot or riding; he saw him and recognized him, while no one else among the Children of Israel saw him. And by his saying: **فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُها** ("‘I took a handful from the messenger’s tracks and flung it’"), he meant he took a handful of dust from Gabriel's track or from the track of Gabriel's horse—the "messenger" being Gabriel—and flung it, i.e., cast the handful onto the molten jewelry, and the calf became alive and had a lowing sound!

The greatest objection to this is the contradiction of these narrations—which will be presented in the following discussion on traditions—with the Book. For His speech explicitly states that the calf was a "body" (جسداً) with a lowing sound, and a "body" is a corpse that has no soul and no life in it; the term is never applied to a body possessed of soul and life. This is in addition to the various objections raised against the narrations that will be mentioned in the upcoming discussion.

It is reported from Abu Muslim regarding the interpretation of the verse that he said: "There is no explicit statement in the Quran for what they mentioned. There is another view, which is that the 'messenger' refers to Moses (عليه السلام), and his 'track' is his Sunnah and the way he commanded and followed. A man might say, 'So-and-so follows the track of so-and-so' if he complies with his way."

The explanation of the verse based on this is that when Moses (عليه السلام) turned to the Samiri with blame and questioned him about the matter that prompted him to lead the people astray with the calf, he said: **بَصُرْتُ بِما لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ** ("‘I saw what they did not see’"), meaning: "I realized that what the people were on was not the truth. I had taken a handful from your track, i.e., something of your religion, then I flung it, i.e., I cast it away and did not adhere to it." His referring to Moses in the third person is like one who addresses a prince saying, "What is the prince's opinion on such-and-her?" His calling him "messenger" would be a type of mockery, as he was a disbeliever who denied him, similar to His saying recounting the disbelievers: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ** — "‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy!’" (15:6). It is clear that the lowing of the calf in this view was due to a mechanical cause, unlike the previous view.

The objection to this is that the context of the verse testifies to the flinging being a consequence of the taking, and the taking a consequence of the seeing. What he mentioned would necessitate the flinging being a consequence of the seeing, and the seeing a consequence of the taking. If what he mentioned were true, it would have been necessary to say: "I saw what they did not see, so I flung what I had taken from the messenger's track," or to say: "I took a handful from the messenger's track, then I saw what they did not see, so I flung it."

Secondly: His interpretation necessitates that His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِي نَفْسِي** ("‘That is how my soul prompted me’") be an allusion to the cause of making the calf and an answer to Moses's question: **فَما خَطْبُكَ** ("‘And what is your business?’"). The gist would be that he only made it because of a prompting from his soul to lead the people astray. Thus, the beginning of the verse would imply he was not a monotheist, and its end would imply he was not an idolater—neither monotheist nor idolater—whereas what is recounted of Moses's subsequent words: **وَانْظُرْ إِلى إِلهِكَ الَّذِي ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفاً لَنُحَرِّقَنَّهُ** ("‘and look at your god to whom you have been devoted: we will surely burn it...’") indicates he was an idolater.

Thirdly: Referring to Moses in the third person while he is being addressed is far-fetched.

Another meaning for the verse can be conceived based on what some mentioned: that the "loads of jewelry" they carried were gold ornaments from the Copts that Moses had commanded them to carry, and they belonged to Moses or were attributed to him—this being what is meant by "the messenger's track." The Samiri describes what he made as being due to his insight in the matter of dyeing and molding; he was skilled in the making of statues in a way the people were not. So his soul prompted him to make for them a golden statue of a calf. He took a handful from the "messenger's track"—the golden jewelry—and flung it into the fire and produced for them a calf, a body with a lowing sound. Its lowing was due to air entering the hollow of its interior and exiting from its mouth under pressure through a mechanical arrangement.

There remains the issue of referring to Moses as "the messenger" while addressing him, calling the people's jewelry "the messenger's track," and calling the making of the calf—which he worshipped—a "soul's prompting."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ فَاذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِي الْحَياةِ أَنْ تَقُولَ لا مِساسَ وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِداً لَنْ تُخْلَفَهُ** — "[Moses] said, ‘Begone! It shall be your [lot] in this life to say, “Do not touch me!” and you have a tryst that you will not fail.’" (20:97). This is his punishment from Moses (عليه السلام) after the crime was established.

His saying: **قالَ فَاذْهَبْ** ("‘Begone!’") is a decree of expulsion from society, such that he does not mix with the people, nor does he touch anyone, nor does anyone touch him by taking, giving, sheltering, companionship, speaking, or any other manifestation of human social life. This is among the most severe types of punishment. His saying: **فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِي الْحَياةِ أَنْ تَقُولَ لا مِساسَ** ("‘It shall be your [lot] in this life to say, “Do not touch me!”’")—the gist of which is that it is decreed and certain for you to live alone as long as you are alive—is a metaphor for his continuous anguish from loneliness and alienation.

It has been said: This was a prayer from Moses against him, and that following his prayer, he was afflicted with a barren disease such that no one approached him except that they were struck with a severe fever. So he would say to whoever approached him: "No touching! No touching!" It has also been said: He was afflicted with obsessive-compulsive disorder (وسواس) such that he became alienated and would flee from everyone he met, crying out: "No touching!" This is a plausible view if the report is authentic.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِداً لَنْ تُخْلَفَهُ** ("‘and you have a tryst that you will not fail’"). Its apparent meaning is an announcement of his destruction at a time Allah has appointed and decreed as an inevitable judgment. It could also be a prayer against him. It has been said: What is meant is the punishment of the hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَانْظُرْ إِلى إِلهِكَ الَّذِي ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفاً لَنُحَرِّقَنَّهُ ثُمَّ لَنَنْسِفَنَّهُ فِي الْيَمِّ نَسْفاً** — "‘and look at your god to whom you have been devoted: we will surely burn it and then scatter its [ashes] into the sea.’" (20:97). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "A person *nasafa* (نسف) food when he winnows it with a winnowing fan to make the husks fly off." End quote.

His saying: **وَانْظُرْ إِلى إِلهِكَ الَّذِي ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفاً** ("‘and look at your god to whom you have been devoted’"), i.e., you remained and persisted in being devoted to it. This indicates that he had taken it as a god for himself to worship.

His saying: **لَنُحَرِّقَنَّهُ ثُمَّ لَنَنْسِفَنَّهُ فِي الْيَمِّ نَسْفاً** ("‘we will surely burn it and then scatter its [ashes] into the sea’"), i.e., I swear we shall burn it with fire and then scatter it in the sea. The mention of burning it has been used as evidence that it was an animal with flesh and blood; for if it were gold, burning it would have no meaning. This supports the previous majority interpretation that it became a living animal with a soul by the casting of the dust taken from Gabriel's track. However, the truth is that it only indicates it was not pure gold, nothing more.

Some have suggested that "we will surely burn it" (لَنُحَرِّقَنَّهُ) is from the "filing" (حرق) of iron when it is filed with a file. The meaning would be: "We shall surely file it with a file and then scatter its filings in the sea." This is more appropriate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّما إِلهُكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** — "‘Your god is only Allah, besides whom there is no god. He embraces all things in [His] knowledge.’" (20:98). It appears this is the conclusion of Moses's (عليه السلام) speech, addressing the Samiri and the Children of Israel. He established with this speech Allah's uniqueness in His divinity, such that no other—whether a calf or any supposed partner—shares it with Him. In its context, it is an elegant argument; he argued that He, the Exalted, is Allah, therefore there is no god but He, and thereby, there is no other god for them.

It has been said: In His saying: **وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** ("‘He embraces all things in [His] knowledge’") there is evidence that the non-existent is called a "thing" because it is known. This contains a fallacy; for the meaning of the verse is that everything called a "thing" is embraced by His knowledge, not that everything embraced by His knowledge is called a "thing." What would benefit the claimant is the second, not the first.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain to Hamza ibn al-Rabi' from someone who mentioned it, he said: "I was in the assembly of Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) when 'Amr ibn 'Ubayd entered and said to him: 'May I be your ransom, the saying of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِي فَقَدْ هَوى** ("and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes")—what is that wrath?' Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) replied: 'It is the punishment, O 'Amr. Indeed, whoever claims that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, moves from one thing to another has described Him with the attribute of a created being. Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is not provoked by anything, nor does anything change Him.'"

**I say:** Al-Tabarsi narrated something similar in *al-Ihtijaj* as a disconnected report.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Layla from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, accepts only righteous deeds, and Allah accepts only the fulfillment of conditions and covenants. So whoever fulfills Allah's condition and employs what He described in His covenant attains what is with Him and completes His promise. Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, informed the servants of the paths of guidance (الهدى), established for them landmarks therein, and informed them how to travel. He said: **وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** ('Indeed I am most forgiving toward him who repents, believes, and acts righteously, and then follows guidance')—and He said: **إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ** ('Allah only accepts from the God-conscious'). So whoever is God-conscious (اتقى الله) in what He commanded meets Allah as a believer in what Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) brought."

In *al-Majma'*: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "**ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** ('and then follows guidance')—to our guardianship (ولايتنا), the People of the House (أهل البيت). By Allah, if a man were to worship Allah his entire life between the Corner and the Station (الركن والمقام), then died without coming with our guardianship, Allah would cast him into the Fire on his face." It was narrated by al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani with his chain, and al-'Ayyashi cited it in his *tafsir* through several paths.

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Kafi* with its chain from Sudayr from him (peace be upon him). In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain from al-Harith ibn 'Umar from him (peace be upon him). In *Manaqib Ibn Shahrashub*, from Abu al-Jarud and Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and from Abu Hamza from al-Sajjad (peace be upon him): similar to it, and the wording is: "to us, the People of the House."

The meaning of guardianship (الولاية) in the hadith is the guardianship over the affairs of the people in their religion and their world. It is the authority (المرجعية) in taking the knowledge of religion and its laws, and in managing the affairs of society. This belonged to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as the Book explicitly states in verses like: **النَّبِيُّ أَوْلى بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ** ("The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves"). Then it was granted to his progeny, the People of his House, after him in the Book through verses like the Verse of Guardianship (آية الولاية) and through what has been narrated from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in mutawatir reports like the Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين), the Hadith of Position (حديث المنزلة), and their likes.

The verse, although situated among verses addressing the Children of Israel and apparently referring to them, is not restricted by anything that makes it exclusive to them or prevents its application to others. It applies to others just as it applies to them. As for its application to them, it is because Moses, by virtue of being an Imam in his nation, possessed the same type of guardianship as other prophets; thus, his nation had to be guided by him and come under his guardianship. As for its application to others, it is because the verse is general and not specific to one people over another. It guides the people in the time of the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to his guardianship, and after him to the guardianship of the Imams from the People of his House (peace be upon them). Guardianship is of one type and carries its meaning regardless of whom it is attributed to.

Once you understand what has preceded, the invalidity of what al-Alusi mentioned in his commentary *Ruh al-Ma'ani* becomes clear. After quoting the previous narration of *Majma' al-Bayan* from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "You know that their guardianship and love (رضي الله عنهم) is something whose obligation we do not dispute. However, interpreting 'guidance' in the verse as that, while it is a narration of what Allah the Exalted addressed to the Children of Israel in the time of Moses (peace be upon him), necessitates saying that Allah the Mighty and Majestic informed the Children of Israel of the People of the House and obligated their guardianship upon them at that time—and this has not been established in authentic reports." End of the relevant part of his speech.

What caused him to fall into this was his interpreting guardianship (الولاية) as "love" (المحبة), then taking the verse as specific to the Children of Israel until he concluded the meaning he mentioned. Guardianship in its verses and reports does not mean love; rather, it is the authority of management and disposal in affairs (ملك التدبير والتصرف في الأمور), which entails the necessity of following and the obligation of obedience. This is what the Imams of the People of the House claim for themselves. As for love, it is an extended meaning of guardianship in its true sense, and one of its usual concomitants; it is what the evidence for the "affection for the near kin" (مودة ذي القربى) indicates directly, whether in verse or narration.

Guardianship of the People of the House (peace be upon them) has a third meaning: that Allah takes charge of the affair of His servant, so He becomes the manager of his affairs and the disposer of his concerns due to his sincerity in servitude. This guardianship belongs to Allah primarily; He is the Guardian (الولي), and there is no guardian besides Him. It is attributed to the People of the House (peace be upon them) because they are the foremost among the nation in opening this door. This is also an extension in attribution, just as the "straight path" (الصراط المستقيم) is attributed to Him primarily in His speech, and to those whom Allah has blessed—the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous—through a type of extension.

In summary, guardianship in the hadith of *al-Majma'* means the authority of management, and the noble verse is general, applying to those other than the Children of Israel just as it applies to them. The Imam (peace be upon him) only interpreted "guidance to guardianship" from the perspective of the verse's application in this nation, which is the determined meaning.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying: **فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنْ بَعْدِكَ**, he said: "We tested them after you. **وَأَضَلَّهُمُ السَّامِرِيُّ**—through the calf they worshipped."

The cause of that was that when Allah promised Moses to send down the Torah and the Tablets to him after thirty days, he informed the Children of Israel of that and went to the appointment, leaving his brother in charge of his people. When the thirty days passed and Moses did not return to them, they rebelled and wanted to kill Aaron. They said, "Moses lied and fled from us." Then Iblis came to them in the form of a man and said to them, "Moses has fled from you and will never return to you. So collect your jewelry for me so that I may make for you a god for you to worship."

The Samiri was at the vanguard of Moses's people on the day Allah drowned Pharaoh and his companions. He saw Gabriel, who was on a creature in the form of a mare (رمكة). Every time it placed its hoof on a spot of the earth, that spot would move. The Samiri saw him—and he was among the best of Moses's companions—so he took the dust from the hoof of Gabriel's mare, and it was moving. He tied it in a pouch and kept it with him, boasting of it to the Children of Israel. When Iblis came to them and they took the calf, he said to the Samiri, "Bring the dust you have." The Samiri brought it and cast it into the interior of the calf. When the dust fell into its interior, it moved, lowed, and fur and hair grew upon it. So the Children of Israel prostrated to it, and the number of those who prostrated to it was seventy thousand from the Children of Israel. Aaron said to them as Allah recounted: **يا قَوْمِ إِنَّما فُتِنْتُمْ بِهِ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ الرَّحْمنُ فَاتَّبِعُونِي وَأَطِيعُوا أَمْرِي**. They replied: **لَنْ نَبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفِينَ حَتَّى يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنا مُوسى**. They intended to harm Aaron, so he fled from them, and they remained in that state until Moses's appointment of forty nights was completed.

On the tenth day of Dhu al-Hijjah, Allah sent down the knowledge of the Tablets containing the Torah and what was needed of rulings, conduct, and stories. Allah revealed to Moses: "Indeed We have tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray; they have worshipped the calf and it has a lowing sound." He said: "O Lord, the calf is from the Samiri, but from whom is the lowing?" He replied: "From Me, O Moses. When I saw them turn away from Me to the calf, I wished to increase their trial."

**فَرَجَعَ مُوسى**—as Allah recounted—**إِلى قَوْمِهِ غَضْبانَ أَسِفاً قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَلَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْداً حَسَناً أَفَطالَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْعَهْدُ أَمْ أَرَدْتُمْ أَنْ يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُمْ مَوْعِدِي**. Then he threw the Tablets and seized his brother's beard and head, pulling him toward himself. He said: **ما مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوا أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِي؟**. Aaron replied—as Allah recounted—: **يَا بْنَ أُمَّ لا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِي وَلا بِرَأْسِي إِنِّي خَشِيتُ أَنْ تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِي**.

The Children of Israel said to him: **ما أَخْلَفْنا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنا**. He said: "We did not oppose you. **وَلكِنَّا حُمِّلْنا أَوْزاراً مِنْ زِينَةِ الْقَوْمِ**—meaning from their jewelry—**فَقَذَفْناها**." He said: "The dust that the Samiri brought, we threw into its interior." Then the Samiri produced the calf and it had a lowing sound. Moses said to him: **فَما خَطْبُكَ يا سامِرِيُّ؟**. The Samiri said: **بَصُرْتُ بِما لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ**—meaning from under the hoof of Gabriel's mare in the sea—**فَنَبَذْتُها**—meaning I kept it—**وَكَذلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِي نَفْسِي**—meaning it beautified it.

Moses produced the calf, burned it with fire, and threw it into the sea. Then Moses said to the Samiri: **فَاذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِي الْحَياةِ أَنْ تَقُولَ لا مِساسَ**—meaning as long as you are alive, and your progeny shall have this sign established among you: that you say "No touching!" so that they know you are Samiris and the people are not deceived by you. They are known to this hour in Egypt and Syria as "No touching." Then Moses intended to kill the Samiri, but Allah revealed to him: "Do not kill him, O Moses, for he is generous." So Moses said to him: **انْظُرْ إِلى إِلهِكَ الَّذِي ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفاً لَنُحَرِّقَنَّهُ ثُمَّ لَنَنْسِفَنَّهُ فِي الْيَمِّ نَسْفاً ، إِنَّما إِلهُكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً**.

**I say:** The apparent meaning of what we have quoted is that his saying: "The cause of that..." etc., is not a continuation of the narration at the beginning of the speech "He said: through the calf they worshipped." Rather, it is from the words of al-Qummi, which he adapted from the reports of others, as is his habit in most of what he cited in his commentary regarding the occasions of revelation (شأن النزول). There are evidences for this within the story he mentioned. Yes, his saying in the middle of the story: "He said: We did not oppose you" is a narration, as is his saying: "He said: The dust that the Samiri brought, we threw into its interior," and his saying: "Then Moses intended..." etc., is the content of a narration reported from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

Furthermore, assuming it is a narration and a completion of the previous one, it is a disconnected (مرسلة) and anonymous (مضمرة) report.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Al-Faryabi, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—reported from 'Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "When Moses hastened to his Lord, the Samiri set to work. He collected what he could of the jewelry of the Children of Israel and molded it into a calf. Then he cast the handful into its interior, and suddenly it was a calf, a body with a lowing sound. The Samiri said to them: 'This is your god and the god of Moses.' Aaron said to them: 'O my people, did your Lord not give you a good promise?'" (the hadith).

**I say:** What is attributed here as a saying to Aaron is recounted by the Quran as the words of Moses (peace be upon him).

In it, Ibn Jarir reported from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: "When Pharaoh and his companions charged into the sea—and Pharaoh was on a black stallion—the stallion hesitated to plunge into the sea. Then Gabriel appeared to him on a mare. When the stallion saw her, he charged after her. The Samiri recognized Gabriel because his mother, fearing he would be slaughtered, had left him in a cave and closed it over him. Gabriel used to come to him and feed him with his fingers—in one milk, in another honey, and in another butter. He continued to feed him until he grew up. When he saw him in the sea, he recognized him. So he took a handful from the track of his horse. He said: He took a handful from under the hoof, and it was cast into the Samiri's heart: 'You shall not cast it upon anything and say "Be such-and-such" except that it shall be.'

The handful remained with him in his hand until they crossed the sea. When Moses and the Children of Israel crossed the sea, Allah drowned the house of Pharaoh. Moses said to his brother Aaron: 'Take my place among my people, and reform, and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption.' Moses went to the appointment with his Lord. The Children of Israel had jewelry from the jewelry of the house of Pharaoh. They felt sinful because of it, so they brought it out for the fire to descend and consume it. When they collected it, the Samiri did this with the handful—he flung it into it and said: 'Be a calf, a body with a lowing sound.' So it became a calf, a body with a lowing sound. The wind would enter from its rear and exit from its mouth, and a sound would be heard from it. He said: 'This is your god and the god of Moses.' So they became devoted to the calf, worshipping it. Aaron said: 'O my people! You are only being tried by it. Indeed your Lord is the All-beneficent. So follow me and obey my command.' They said: 'We will not stop being devoted to it until Moses returns to us.'"

**I say:** The report—as you see—does not include the hoof's dust having the property of giving life. However, it contains something greater: its having the property of the word of creation (كلمة التكوين). According to this, the Samiri only used it to make the jewelry emerge from the fire in the form of a calf, a body with a lowing sound, so it emerged as he wished without a normal natural cause. As for life, there is no mention of it in the report; rather, the explicit mention of the wind entering its interior and exiting with a sound indicates it was not characterized by life.

Furthermore, the accounts mentioning that the mother of the Samiri (السامري) hid him in a cave when he was born out of fear that Pharaoh (فرعون) would slaughter him, and that Gabriel (جبريل) used to come to him and feed him from his fingers until he grew up, are not to be relied upon. Moreover, the Samiri being from the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل) is not established; rather, Ibn Abbas himself denied it in the report of Sa'id ibn Jubayr, which details the story, and it was narrated from Ibn Abi Hatim on the authority of Ibn Abbas that he was from the people of Kerman (كرمان).

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from al-Suddi, who said: "Moses set out toward his Lord and spoke to Him. When He spoke to him, He said to him: 'What has caused you to leave your people in haste, O Moses?' He said: 'They are there, close on my heels, and I hastened to You, my Lord, that You may be pleased.' He said: 'Indeed, We have tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray.' When He informed him of their news, Moses said: 'O Lord, this Samiri—he commanded them to take the calf; but did You see the spirit (الروح), who breathed it into it?' The Lord said: 'I did.' He said: 'O Lord, then it was You who led them astray.'"

Then Moses returned to his people, angry and sorrowful (أسفا). He said: "Grieved." **(He said, ‘O my people! Did not your Lord give you a true promise?** — until His saying — **We did not break our tryst with you of our own accord’)** [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Ta-Ha, Ayah 86-87]. He says: "By our own power." **(‘but we were laden with the fardels of the people’s ornaments’)**—meaning the jewelry of the Copts (القبط)—**(‘and we cast them [into the fire], and so did the Samiri throw.’ Then he produced for them a calf, a [mere] body with a low, and they stood by it [in worship].)** It used to low and walk. Then Aaron (هارون) said to them: **(‘O my people! You are only being tested by it’)**—meaning you are being tried (ابتليتم) by the calf. He said: **(‘What is your business, O Samiri?’)**—meaning what is the matter with you—until His saying: **(‘and look at your god to whom you have been cleaving. We will surely burn it.’)**

He said: "So he took it and slaughtered it—then he burned it with a file, meaning he ground it down, then he scattered it into the sea (اليم). Not a single flowing river remained that day except that some of it fell into it. Then Moses said to them: 'Drink from it.' So they drank. Whoever loved it, gold appeared upon his mustache. That is when He says: **(and they were made to drink the [love of the] calf into their hearts because of their faithlessness)** [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah al-Baqarah, Ayah 93]." (The tradition).

**I say:** Among the strange things contained in this story is the growth of gold on the mustaches of those who loved the calf as a result of drinking the water, and interpreting the saying of the Exalted: **(and they were made to drink the [love of the] calf into their hearts because of their faithlessness)** based upon it. The phrasing "into their hearts" (فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ) is indeed the best evidence that what is meant by "making them drink" (الإشراب) is the settling of its love and its permeation into their hearts, rather than the drinking of water into which the calf was scattered after being ground down.

Even stranger is his combining its slaughtering and its grinding. Slaughtering (الذبح) only occurs in an animal possessing flesh and blood, while grinding (السحك) and filing (البرد) are only feasible in a body cast from gold or another metal.

In the same source, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "When Gabriel descended and ascended with Moses to the heaven, the Samiri perceived him from among the people and took a handful from the track of the horse. Gabriel carried Moses behind him until he drew near the gate of heaven. He ascended, and Allah wrote the Tablets (الألواح) while he [Moses] heard the scratching of the pens on the Tablets. When He informed him that his people had been tried after him, Moses descended, took the calf, and burned it."

**I say:** This contains what is even stranger than the preceding accounts, namely the ascension of Gabriel with Moses to the heaven. The context of the verses of the story in this Surah and others does not support this. Even stranger is the taking of dust from the hoofprint of Gabriel's horse when he descended to ascend with Moses, while Moses was at the Mount (الطور) and the Samiri was with the Children of Israel. If this descent and ascent were true, they would have occurred at the end of the appointed time (الميقات), whereas the Samiri's leading the Children of Israel astray occurred days before that.

A similar problem applies to all other reports that mention his taking the soil from under the hoof of Gabriel's horse when he appeared to Pharaoh until his horse entered the sea. For Pharaoh and his companions only entered the sea after the Children of Israel—and with them the Samiri, if he were there—had exited the sea, considering the distance of the sea's width. So where was the Samiri in relation to Pharaoh?

The greatest objection to these reports—as previously alluded to—is: First, they contradict the Book (الكتاب), as the Book explicitly states that the calf was a body (جسدا) without a spirit, while these reports affirm for it a body possessed of life and spirit. A report (الخبر) has no probative force (حجية), even if it is technically "authentic" (صحيح), if it contradicts the Book. Were it not so, the Book would lose its probative force upon contradicting a report, and the probative force of the Book would then depend on the report's agreement or lack of contradiction. This is while the probative force of the report—rather, the very word of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) which the report narrates, and indeed the prophethood (النبوة) of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) itself—depends on the probative force of the apparent meaning (ظاهر) of the Book. This is a manifest circularity (دور), and the full discussion belongs to the science of principles (علم الأصول).

Second: These are solitary reports (أخبار آحاد), and there is no meaning to establishing the probative force of solitary reports in matters other than legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية). The reality of legislative establishment (الجعل التشريعي) is the obligation to apply the effects of reality to the apparent proof (الحجة الظاهرية), which depends on the existence of a practical effect (أثر عملي) for that proof, as is the case with rulings. As for other matters, there is no effect to be arranged upon the establishment of probative force. For example, if a narration states that the *Basmalah* (البسملة) is part of the Surah, the meaning of establishing its probative force is the obligation to recite the *Basmalah* during the prayer. However, if it is narrated, for instance, that the Samiri was a man from Kerman—which is a speculative solitary report—the meaning of establishing its probative force would be to turn the speculation (الظن) regarding its content into certainty (قطع), which is an ontological decree (حكم تكويني) that is impossible and has nothing to do with legislation (التشريع). The full discussion is in the science of principles.

Some who do not accept the majority's interpretation of the verse based on the content of these reports have raised other poor objections against them, and some of their defenders have responded with even poorer arguments.

Some have supported the aforementioned interpretation by claiming it is an interpretation based on transmitted reports (تفسير بالمأثور) from the best of generations—the first generation, the generation of the Companions (الصحابة) and the Successors (التابعين)—and it is not something said based on personal opinion (الرأي). Thus, it has the status of a "raised" report (خبر مرفوع) [attributed to the Prophet], and deviating from it is misguidance.

To this we say, first: The fact that a certain generation is the "best of generations" does not necessitate the probative force of every statement traced back to it. There is no necessary correlation between the goodness of a generation and every statement within it being true and right, every opinion being correct, and every deed being righteous. Among the reports transmitted from them is a vast quantity of contradictory statements and conflicting narrations. Plain reason (صريح العقل) dictates the falsehood of one of two contradictories and the mendacity of one of two conflicting reports. It obliges the critical researcher to demand proof (الحجة) for their statements just as he demands it from others; they have their merit in what they excelled in.

Second: The fact that the subject matter of the transmitted report is something not subject to personal opinion—such as the details of stories—only makes their report equivalent to a "raised" report (مرفوع) if they ultimately trace their narrations back to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). However, we find that even the Companions frequently narrate reports that trace back to the Jews and others. This is beyond doubt for anyone who reviews the transmitted reports regarding the stories of Dhu al-Qarnayn (ذي القرنين), the Garden of Iram (جنة إرم), the story of Moses and al-Khidr (الخضر), the giants (العمالقة), the miracles of Moses, and what has been narrated regarding the "slips" of the prophets and other such countless matters. Thus, their being in the status of "raised" reports does not necessitate their being raised to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

Third: Even if we concede they are in the status of "raised" reports, such reports—even the authentic (صحيح) ones—carry no probative force in matters other than legal rulings, especially those that contradict the Book, as previously mentioned.

In *al-Mahasin*, with its chain of transmission from al-Wassafi, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Among the things Allah whispered to Moses was His saying: 'O Lord, this Samiri made the calf, but from whom did the lowing (الخوار) come?' Then Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, revealed to him: 'That was My trial (فتنتي), so do not investigate it.'"

**I say:** This meaning is found in various narrations with different wordings. The primary reason for this is the prevalence of "narration by meaning" (النقل بالمعنى), especially in Prophetic traditions, due to the prohibition of writing Hadith in the first century AH. Consequently, some narrators cast it into the mold of predestination (الجبر), though it is not so; rather, it is a misguidance of retribution (إضلال مجازاة) and not an initial misguidance (إضلال ابتدائي). He has frequently attributed this type of misguidance to Himself in His Book, as He said: **(He misleads many by it and guides many by it, and He misleads no one by it except the transgressors)** [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah al-Baqarah, Ayah 26].

The best expression of the meaning of this misguidance in the narrations is what was previously mentioned in the narration of al-Qummi: "He—meaning Moses—said: 'O Lord, the calf was from the Samiri, but from whom was the lowing?' He said: 'From Me, O Moses. When I saw them turn away from Me toward the calf, I wished to increase their trial (فتنة).'"

And what occurred in the narration of Rashid ibn Sa'd, quoted in *al-Durr al-Manthur*: "He said: 'O Lord, who placed the spirit in it?' He said: 'I did.' He said: 'Then You, O Lord, led them astray!' He said: 'O Moses, O head of the prophets and O father of the judges, I saw that [desire] in their hearts, so I facilitated it for them.'" (The tradition).

In *al-Majma'*, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "Moses intended to kill the Samiri, but Allah, the Glorified, revealed to him: 'Do not kill him, O Moses, for he is generous (سخي).'"
